title: Conversational UI Patterns
description: Learn the fundamental patterns for designing conversational interfaces — from chat UX to voice interactions to multimodal experiences.
learningObjectives: Identify and apply the core structural elements of conversational interfaces including message types, input mechanisms, and visual hierarchy
Design conversation flows that guide users effectively using linear, branching, and open-ended patterns
Choose the right input mechanisms and message formats for different conversational contexts and user needs

sections.title: Anatomy of a Conversation UI
sections.content:
A conversation UI looks simple on the surface — messages go back and forth. But beneath that simplicity is a carefully designed system of visual hierarchy, timing, and spatial relationships that make the interaction feel natural.

**The Message Thread**

The message thread is the backbone of any conversational interface. It's where the entire interaction history lives, and its design choices affect how users perceive and navigate the conversation.

The most fundamental requirement is **visual distinction between participants**. Users need to instantly tell their messages apart from the AI's responses, and this is typically achieved through alignment (user messages on the right, AI on the left), color (different bubble colors), or both. The key is consistency — once you establish a visual language, never break it.

Timestamps and grouping also deserve careful thought. Not every message needs a visible timestamp. A better approach is to group messages sent within a short window and show a single timestamp for the group. For longer conversations, date separators like "Today" and "Yesterday" help users orient themselves within the history.

Scroll behavior is another area that seems trivial but has real impact on usability. New messages should auto-scroll to the bottom, but only when the user is already at the bottom of the thread. If they've scrolled up to review earlier messages, auto-scrolling becomes disruptive. In that situation, show a "New messages" indicator instead and let the user choose when to jump down.

**The Input Area**

The input area is where users compose their messages, and its design signals what kind of input the system expects.

Consider **text input sizing** first. A single-line input suggests short queries, while a multi-line text area invites longer, more detailed messages. Match the input size to your use case: a quick-answer bot gets a single line, and a writing assistant gets a text area.

The **send affordance** should be visually prominent and accessible. Support keyboard shortcuts — Enter to send, Shift+Enter for a new line — and make the convention clear. Nothing frustrates users more than accidentally sending half a message.

Finally, **input state indicators** keep the user oriented during the interaction. Show users when the AI is processing their message with a typing indicator, when the input is disabled during generation, and when they can type again. These states prevent the confusion of sending messages into a void.

**The System Layer**

Beyond messages, conversational UIs need system-level elements that frame the interaction.

The **welcome state** is the user's first impression. What do they see before any messages are exchanged? A blank chat feels cold and uninviting. A welcome message with suggested prompts or a brief description of capabilities helps users start confidently and gives them a clear sense of what the AI can do.

**Status indicators** communicate connection status, AI availability, and processing state. These should be visible but not distracting — a subtle status dot or banner is usually sufficient. The goal is to keep users informed without pulling their attention away from the conversation itself.

For multi-conversation interfaces, **conversation management** becomes essential. Users need ways to start new conversations, switch between existing ones, and find past interactions. As the number of conversations grows, search and conversation titles become critical navigation tools.

sections.title: Message Types and Formatting
sections.content:
Conversational interfaces aren't limited to plain text bubbles. Different message types serve different communication needs, and choosing the right format for each piece of information makes the difference between a clear conversation and a confusing wall of text.

**Text Messages**

Text is the foundation of conversational UI, but even plain text benefits from thoughtful formatting.

**Markdown rendering** is one of the most impactful features you can support. Bold, italic, headers, and lists in AI responses make longer messages scannable and organized. Claude naturally produces well-structured text with headers and bullet points, and rendering this formatting properly lets that structure shine through.

If your AI assistant helps with technical tasks, **code blocks** deserve special attention. Render code in monospaced font with syntax highlighting, and always include a "Copy" button — users almost always want to copy code snippets rather than manually selecting them.

**Message length handling** requires deliberate design as well. Very long messages need special treatment. Consider collapsible sections, a "Show more" truncation, or breaking long responses into multiple message bubbles. A 2,000-word response crammed into a single bubble is overwhelming and discourages careful reading.

**Rich Media Messages**

Some information is better communicated through structured visual elements rather than text. Here are the most common rich message types and when each one works best:

• **Cards** — Structured containers with an image, title, description, and action buttons. Ideal for product recommendations, search results, or any content with a consistent structure.
• **Carousels** — Horizontally scrollable sets of cards, useful when presenting multiple options like "Here are 5 restaurants near you." Keep carousels short; more than 7-8 items becomes unwieldy.
• **Tables** — Perfect for data comparisons or structured information. "Product A costs $10 and has 4.5 stars" reads better as a table when you're comparing multiple products side by side.
• **Images and media** — When the AI references visual content, embed it directly in the conversation rather than linking out. Inline images, charts, and diagrams maintain conversational flow and keep users from context-switching.

**Interactive Messages**

Some messages invite user action beyond reading.

**Forms within messages** allow you to embed short inputs — a rating scale, a date picker, a dropdown — directly in the conversation thread. This approach is faster and more natural than asking users to type structured information in free text. Rather than three separate "What is your..." questions, a single embedded form collects everything at once.

**Confirmation messages** serve a critical safety role. When the AI is about to take an action, showing a confirmation card with the action details and "Confirm" / "Cancel" buttons protects users from unintended consequences. This pattern is especially important for any destructive or irreversible operation where a mistake could be costly.

After completing a task, **feedback prompts** give users a lightweight way to rate the interaction. A thumbs up/down or a single-question survey embedded in the conversation captures signal without interrupting the user's workflow.

sections.title: Input Mechanisms
sections.content:
How users provide input shapes the entire conversational experience. The right input mechanism reduces friction, guides users toward successful interactions, and prevents the blank-page paralysis that open-ended text boxes can create.

**Free Text Input**

Open text input is the most flexible but also the most intimidating input mechanism. It works best for experienced users, complex queries, or creative tasks where you can't predict what the user wants to say. It's the natural default for general-purpose assistants, but it needs careful supporting design to work well.

The **placeholder text** in your input field is a design opportunity that's easy to overlook. "Ask me anything" is vague and unhelpful. Compare that with "Ask about your sales data, team performance, or upcoming deadlines" — specific and actionable. The placeholder should answer the question every user silently asks: "What should I type here?"

**Input validation and guidance** also matter more than you might expect. If your AI feature expects a certain type of input, validate before sending. If a user pastes a 50,000-word document into a field that handles 1,000 words, catch that before it fails on the backend. A clear, helpful error message at the input stage is far better than a cryptic failure after the user has already waited.

**Suggested Responses and Quick Replies**

Suggested responses are pre-written options that users can tap instead of typing. They shine in several situations: when there's a predictable set of responses like "Yes / No / Tell me more," when users are on mobile where typing is slower, or when you want to guide users toward productive interactions without constraining them.

Design suggestions as horizontally scrollable chips or vertically stacked buttons, and keep the text short — two to five words per suggestion is the sweet spot. Always remove suggestions once the user makes a choice or types their own response, so the conversation doesn't accumulate stale options.

The most effective suggestions are **dynamic**, changing based on context. After Claude answers a question, offering follow-up suggestions based on what the user is likely to ask next — "Go deeper," "Give me an example," "How does this compare to...?" — creates a guided experience that still feels conversational.

**Structured Input**

Sometimes you need specific, structured information from users, and free text is the wrong tool for the job.

**Inline forms** are the right choice when the AI needs specific data points like a date range, a budget, or a location. Embedding a small form in the conversation is far better than asking users to type each field separately. Three separate messages asking for departure city, arrival city, and dates is clunky and slow compared to a single flight search form.

**File and image upload** should feel seamless when your AI feature can process documents, images, or data files. Support drag-and-drop, paste from clipboard, and file picker — all three. Show a preview of what was uploaded so users can confirm they selected the right file before the AI processes it.

For mobile or hands-free contexts, **voice input** adds an important accessibility dimension. Show the transcription in real time so users can catch errors before sending, and always provide a text fallback. Voice input in a quiet office or a crowded bus isn't always practical, and users should never feel locked into a single input mode.

**Choosing the Right Mechanism**

The best conversational interfaces don't commit to a single input type. Instead, they mix input mechanisms based on context. Start a conversation with suggested prompts to lower the barrier to entry, switch to free text for the main interaction where flexibility matters most, and use quick replies for follow-up decisions where the options are clear. The input mechanism should adapt to the conversation, not force the conversation to adapt to the input.

sections.title: Conversation Flow Patterns
sections.content:
The flow of a conversation — how it moves from one state to the next — is the invisible architecture of conversational design. Different tasks call for different flow patterns, and choosing the wrong pattern makes even a capable AI feel frustrating to use.

**Linear Flows**

Linear flows move through a predefined sequence of steps, one at a time. They work best for onboarding, form completion, step-by-step wizards, or any process with a fixed sequence. "First, tell me your name. Next, what role are you in? Finally, what are you hoping to learn?" is a classic linear pattern.

When designing linear flows, show progress through the sequence (step 2 of 5), allow going back to previous steps, and keep each step focused on a single question or action. Don't ask three things at once — that defeats the purpose of breaking the process into steps. At the same time, know when to break linearity. If a user's answer to step 2 makes step 3 irrelevant, skip it. Rigid linear flows that ask unnecessary questions feel robotic and waste users' time.

**Branching Flows**

Branching flows change direction based on user responses, creating different paths through the conversation. They're ideal for troubleshooting, decision support, recommendation engines, or any scenario where different inputs lead to fundamentally different outcomes. A question like "Are you having a billing issue or a technical issue?" sends the conversation down entirely different paths.

The design work for branching flows happens before you touch any UI. Map out the decision tree, identify dead ends and loops, and make sure every branch leads somewhere useful. If a branch ends with "I can't help with that," you've designed a trap, not a conversation.

**Graceful branch recovery** is what separates good branching design from great. Users will try to jump between branches. If someone starts down the billing path but then mentions a technical issue, the AI should be able to pivot without forcing the user to start over. Design for the reality that users change their minds mid-conversation.

**Open-Ended Flows**

Open-ended flows have no predetermined path — the conversation goes wherever the user takes it. This pattern works best for general-purpose assistants, creative brainstorming, exploratory research, or any context where you genuinely can't predict what users will want to discuss.

The challenge with open-ended flows is that without structure, users can feel lost. Combat this with suggested topics, conversation starters, and contextual suggestions that appear when the conversation stalls. These gentle nudges provide just enough structure to keep users moving without constraining the interaction.

Maintaining context is the other major challenge. In long open-ended conversations, the AI needs to track what's been discussed. Surface conversation context visually — a sidebar with key topics, a summary of decisions made, or tags that indicate what areas have been covered. This helps both the user and the AI stay oriented.

**Hybrid Flows**

The most effective conversational interfaces blend these patterns based on the moment, rather than committing to one approach for the entire interaction.

A common hybrid is to **start structured and then open up**. An onboarding flow might be linear while gathering user preferences, then transition to open-ended interaction once the AI has enough context to be useful. The inverse also works well: a support conversation might **start open-ended** with "What's going on?" and then narrow into a branching troubleshooting flow once the issue is identified.

Another powerful hybrid is **periodic structure in open conversations**. In a long brainstorming session, the AI might periodically offer to summarize what's been discussed and organize ideas — introducing brief moments of structure into an otherwise open flow. This keeps the conversation productive without sacrificing the creative freedom that makes open-ended interaction valuable.

sections.title: Map a Conversational Flow for a Support Bot
sections.content: Apply conversational flow patterns by designing the conversation architecture for an AI-powered support bot.

exercise.prompt:
You're designing a conversational support bot for a SaaS product that helps users manage their subscriptions. The bot needs to handle three main scenarios:

1. **Billing questions** — Users asking about charges, invoices, and payment methods
2. **Plan changes** — Users wanting to upgrade, downgrade, or cancel their subscription
3. **Technical issues** — Users experiencing bugs or needing help with features

Design the conversational flow by describing:

1. **Welcome state** — What does the user see when they open the support chat? What input mechanisms do you offer (free text, suggestions, or both)?
2. **Intent detection** — How does the conversation determine which of the three scenarios the user needs? Design both a guided path (using suggested responses) and a free-text path.
3. **One complete branch** — Pick one of the three scenarios and map out the full conversation flow including: the questions the bot asks, the decision points, the information it presents, and how the conversation resolves.
4. **Edge cases** — How do you handle: (a) a user whose issue spans two categories, (b) a user who wants to talk to a human, (c) a user who returns to the bot after a previous unresolved conversation?
5. **Message types** — For your complete branch, specify at least three different message types you'd use (text, cards, forms, quick replies, etc.) and explain why each is the right choice for that moment.

Focus on the conversation design, not the visual design. Describe what the bot says, what options it offers, and how the flow moves from state to state.
hints: For the welcome state, consider offering the three main categories as quick-reply buttons while also allowing free text — this covers both users who know what they need and users who aren't sure
Intent detection doesn't have to be binary — if the user types something ambiguous, the bot can ask a clarifying question rather than guessing
For your complete branch, think about what information the bot needs to collect and in what order — don't ask for everything upfront if some questions depend on earlier answers
Edge cases are where good conversational design separates from great — how you handle a user who says 'Actually, never mind, I have a different question' mid-flow shows how resilient your design is
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A welcome state that provides both guided (suggested responses) and unguided (free text) entry points with specific UI copy for the welcome message and suggestions. (2) An intent detection approach that handles both clear and ambiguous user inputs — including what the bot says when it's unsure which category applies. (3) A complete conversation branch with at least 4-5 conversational turns, showing specific bot messages, user response options, and decision points. (4) Thoughtful handling of all three edge cases with specific bot messages and flow transitions. (5) At least three different message types used in the complete branch, each with a clear rationale. Deduct points for generic bot messages that could apply to any product, for flows that don't handle user deviation, or for designs that only use text messages.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Conversational UI design is about creating structured interactions that feel natural.

**Master the anatomy.** Every conversation UI rests on three structural pillars: a well-designed message thread that makes history easy to scan, a thoughtful input area that signals what kind of response the system expects, and a system layer that provides context through welcome states, status indicators, and conversation management. These elements are the foundation that everything else builds on.

**Match message types to content.** Plain text works for explanations, cards present structured data at a glance, quick replies streamline decisions, and inline forms collect specific data without breaking conversational flow. The right message format makes information instantly comprehensible, while the wrong one buries it in a wall of undifferentiated text.

**Design input for the moment.** Start with suggestions to lower the barrier to entry, use free text for complex queries where flexibility matters, and switch to structured input when you need specific data. The input mechanism should guide users toward successful interactions, not constrain them into a single mode of expression.

**Choose flow patterns intentionally.** Linear flows work for step-by-step processes, branching flows for decision-driven interactions, and open-ended flows for exploration and creativity. The best conversational interfaces blend these patterns, transitioning between structure and freedom as the conversation evolves and the user's needs shift.
